meta.title: RTM Billing for Pain Management: Complete CPT Code Guide (2025)
meta.description: How to bill CPT 98975, 98977, 98980, and 98981 for chronic pain patients. Enrollment requirements, documentation standards, and common denial reasons.

hero.h1: RTM Billing for Pain Management: Complete CPT Code Guide
hero.subhead: Remote Therapeutic Monitoring generates $120-150/patient/month for chronic pain practices. Here's exactly how to bill it correctly.

content.h2: What is RTM for Pain Management?
content.body:
Remote Therapeutic Monitoring (RTM) allows pain practices to bill for monitoring non-physiological patient data between visits. Unlike Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM), which requires physiological devices like blood pressure cuffs or pulse oximeters, RTM covers exactly what pain practices already collect: pain scores, functional status, medication adherence, and treatment response.

For chronic pain patients, RTM captures the monitoring work that's already happening — phone calls about pain levels, portal messages about medication side effects, check-in questionnaires about functional status. The difference is documentation and billing.

CMS introduced RTM codes in 2022, and coverage has expanded steadily. Medicare covers RTM nationally. Most commercial payers now cover RTM codes, though specific policies vary.

content.h2: The RTM CPT Codes for Pain Management
content.body:
Four CPT codes apply to RTM billing for pain practices:

**98975 — RTM Device Supply and Setup**
One-time billing code for initial patient enrollment and device/software setup. Covers patient education, app installation, and initial configuration. Bill once per treatment episode (typically once per patient, or once per new treatment plan).

Reimbursement: ~$20-25

**98976 — RTM Device Supply, Respiratory**
Not applicable to pain management. This code is specifically for respiratory RTM.

**98977 — RTM Device Supply, Musculoskeletal**
Monthly billing code for ongoing device/software supply and scheduled data transmission. For pain management, this covers the patient app that collects pain scores, functional status, and medication adherence.

Reimbursement: ~$50-60/month

**98980 — RTM Treatment Management, First 20 Minutes**
Monthly billing code for clinical staff time reviewing RTM data and making treatment decisions. Requires 20 minutes of cumulative time per patient per month. Time can be spread across multiple sessions.

Reimbursement: ~$50-70/month

**98981 — RTM Treatment Management, Additional 20 Minutes**
Billable when clinical time exceeds 40 minutes per patient per month. Less commonly billed in pain management unless patients require intensive monitoring.

Reimbursement: ~$40-50 per additional 20 minutes

**Typical monthly RTM billing per patient:**
98977 + 98980 = $100-130 depending on payer

With 100 chronic pain patients enrolled: $10,000-13,000/month

content.h2: Which Pain Patients Qualify for RTM?
content.body:
RTM is appropriate for patients with chronic conditions requiring ongoing monitoring between visits. In pain management, this includes:

**Strong candidates:**
• Chronic low back pain with ongoing treatment
• Chronic neck pain under management
• Neuropathic pain (diabetic neuropathy, post-herpetic neuralgia)
• Complex regional pain syndrome
• Fibromyalgia
• Failed back surgery syndrome
• Patients on chronic opioid therapy requiring close monitoring
• Post-procedure patients during recovery period

**Requirements for RTM enrollment:**
1. Established patient with chronic condition
2. Treatment plan that benefits from between-visit monitoring
3. Patient consent to participate
4. Patient capable of using monitoring device/app (or caregiver assistance)

**Patients who may not qualify:**
• Acute pain patients (seen once, condition resolves)
• Patients already enrolled in RPM for the same condition
• Patients who cannot or will not participate in data submission

RTM billing requires documentation that supports medical necessity and demonstrates the monitoring actually occurred.

**For 98975 (Initial Setup):**
• Documentation of patient enrollment and consent
• Evidence of device/app setup and patient education
• Treatment plan indicating need for remote monitoring

**For 98977 (Monthly Device Supply):**
• Evidence of data transmission from patient
• Minimum data submission: typically 16 days per month required
• Documentation that patient is actively participating

**For 98980/98981 (Treatment Management):**
• Time log showing cumulative minutes spent reviewing data
• Clinical notes documenting data review and treatment decisions
• Evidence that review was performed by qualified clinical staff
• Treatment modifications or confirmations based on data review

**Common documentation failures:**
• No time tracking (can't prove 20 minutes spent)
• Generic notes ('reviewed RTM data') without specific findings
• No evidence of treatment decisions based on data
• Missing patient consent documentation

A typical RTM workflow in a pain practice:

**Patient Enrollment (Day 1):**
1. Identify chronic pain patients who qualify
2. Obtain consent for RTM participation
3. Set up patient on RTM app/platform
4. Educate patient on data submission expectations
5. Document enrollment, bill 98975

**Ongoing Monitoring (Monthly):**
1. Patient submits pain scores, functional status, medication adherence via app
2. Data aggregates on provider dashboard
3. Clinical staff reviews data (MA, RN, or provider)
4. Time spent reviewing documented automatically or manually
5. Treatment decisions made based on data (continue current plan, adjust medication, schedule visit)
6. At 20+ minutes cumulative time, bill 98980
7. Bill 98977 for ongoing device/app supply

**Monthly Billing:**
• 98977 billed if patient submitted data on 16+ days
• 98980 billed if 20+ minutes of clinical time documented
• Both codes can be billed same month for same patient

content.h2: Common RTM Denials and How to Avoid Them
content.body:
**Denial: 'Service not covered'**
Cause: Payer doesn't recognize RTM codes or has specific coverage policies.
Prevention: Verify RTM coverage before enrollment. Check payer-specific policies. Some payers require prior authorization for RTM.

**Denial: 'Documentation insufficient'**
Cause: Missing time logs, generic notes, no evidence of treatment decisions.
Prevention: Document specific data reviewed, time spent, and clinical decisions. Use structured templates.

**Denial: 'Duplicate billing'**
Cause: Patient also billed for RPM or chronic care management (CCM) for same condition.
Prevention: RTM and RPM cannot both be billed for the same patient in the same month. CCM and RTM can be billed together if services are distinct and documented separately.

**Denial: 'Medical necessity not established'**
Cause: No documentation of why remote monitoring benefits this patient.
Prevention: Include RTM rationale in treatment plan. Document how monitoring data informs treatment.

**Denial: 'Time threshold not met'**
Cause: Less than 20 minutes documented for 98980.
Prevention: Accurate time tracking. Consider using RTM platform with automatic time capture.

content.h2: RTM vs RPM: Which Should Pain Practices Use?
content.body:
Pain practices can bill RTM, RPM, or both (for different patients or different conditions).

**RTM (98975, 98977, 98980, 98981):**
• Covers non-physiological data (pain scores, function, adherence)
• No device requirement — app-based monitoring qualifies
• Lower implementation cost
• Well-suited to pain management workflows

**RPM (99453, 99454, 99457, 99458):**
• Covers physiological data (vital signs, weight, glucose)
• Requires FDA-cleared monitoring device
• Higher reimbursement per patient
• Better suited to practices managing comorbidities (hypertension, diabetes)

**For most pain practices:**
RTM is the better fit. Pain management is fundamentally about monitoring symptoms (pain, function) rather than vital signs. RTM captures exactly what pain practices already track.

Some practices bill RPM for patients with comorbid conditions (e.g., hypertensive patient with chronic pain) and RTM for pain-only patients.

**Step 1: Verify payer coverage**
Contact major payers (Medicare, your top 3-5 commercial payers) to confirm RTM coverage and any specific requirements.

**Step 2: Select RTM platform**
You need a system to capture patient data, track clinical time, and generate documentation. Options range from EHR-integrated modules to standalone RTM platforms like clinIQ.

**Step 3: Develop enrollment workflow**
Identify which staff will enroll patients, how consent is obtained, and how patients are trained on the app.

**Step 4: Establish monitoring workflow**
Define who reviews RTM data (MA, RN, provider), how often, and how time is tracked.

**Step 5: Start with pilot group**
Enroll 20-30 chronic pain patients initially. Work out workflow issues before scaling.

**Step 6: Scale enrollment**
Once workflow is smooth, systematically enroll all qualifying chronic pain patients.

**Expected timeline:**
• Month 1: Platform setup, workflow design, pilot enrollment
• Month 2: First RTM billing, workflow refinement
• Month 3: Scale enrollment to 50-100 patients
• Month 6: Steady-state operations, $10,000-15,000/month revenue
